lTICAL PRACTICES COM ISSl N 
S:tcro~nt:nto 958H 
January 16 I 
1976 
6) 322·5660 
Dominic J. Raciti, City Council 
City of Costa Mesa 
P.O. Box 1200 
Costa Mesa, 
CA 
92626 
Dear Mr. Raciti: 
ED~.'lJ~lD G. BRO\'JN JR, GovC'mor 
Thank you for your letter of December 26, 1975, seeking 
advice concerning your obligations under the Political Reform 
Act. 
Since you do not indicate that you are seeking a formal 
opinion pursuant to Government Code Section 83114, the follow-
ing represents the informal views of the staff. 
Chapter 4 of the Political Reform Act requires candidates 
and committees to report expenditures and contributions made 
or received for political purposes. 
If the two publications 
to which you refer were not published for the purpose of in-
fluencing the voters on behalf of your candidacy, you need not 
report the cost associated with either pUblication. 
If, how-
ever, they were published for a political purpose, you would 
be required to report the cost of the jewelry store advertise-
ment as a campaign expenditure and the cost of the bank adver-
tisement as an in-kind contribution. 
Moreover, if the cost of 
the bank advertisement was more than $500 and this cost w s 
incurred for a political purpose, the bank would be a committee 
within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013(b) and 
required to file campaign statements pursuant to Government 
Code Sections 84200, et seq. 
Any doubt as to whether the pub-
lications were motivated by a political purpose should be re-
solved in favor of concluding that there was such a purpose 
since the relevant statutory provision defines an expenditure 
as any "payment .. , unless it is clear from the surrounding 
circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." 
Government Code Section 82025. 
If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate 
to contact me. 
TP:pvp 
Sincerely, 
f'\ ,. rr \ 
jJJJt./ "-
Ted Prim, 
S-taff Counsel 


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
Staff 
Dote 
I 12/31/75 
Jeanne 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Subject: 
Opinion Request No. 75-222 
No. 75-222, from Dominic J. Raciti, City of Costa Mesa, 
will not be treated as a formal opinion request. 
Please 
cross out the number and dates in the upper right corner. 


CITY OF COSTA MESA 
OPln:J, 
CALIFORNIA 92626 
P. O. BOX 1200 
FROM THE OFFICE OF THE CITY COUNCIL 
December 26, 1975 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 "K" Street 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Attention: 
Nancy Salzman, Counsel 
Dear Ms. Salzman: 
I am enclosing a copy of an advertisement which was paid for 
by South Coast National Bank, of which I am a director. 
The advertisement appeared well before interest was generated 
in the city council race in our city, however, a reading of 
this add mentions my position as a member of the city council. 
In addition, I am enclosing a copy of a brochure recently pub-
lished as an announcement to our many friends and customers of 
a business reorganization which we recently undertook to give 
my son, Robert, a greater responsibility in its operation. 
You 
will note in the material blocked out in red and marked with a 
star that my membership on the city council was mentioned. 
In neither piece of material was there any campaign language or 
any request for support or votes. 
Please advise whether or not all or any portion of the costs of 
these two pieces of material should be declared as campaign con-
tributions in the forthcoming city council election in the City 
of Costa Mesa. 
I am an incumbent and have filed my nomination 
papers for re-election and would appreciate your answer prior to 
the time that it is necessary for me to file any reports with 
respect to political contributions and expenditures. 
Your early attention in this matter will be appreciated. 
DJR:cjr 
Enclosure 
Very truly yours, 
Dominic J. Racit 
City Council

sections.question: I am enclosing a copy of an advertisement which was paid for by South Coast National Bank, of which I am a director. The advertisement appeared well before interest was generated in the city council race in our city, however, a reading of this add mentions my position as a member of the city council. In addition, I am enclosing a copy of a brochure recently published as an announcement to our many friends and customers of a business reorganization which we recently undertook to give my son, Robert, a greater responsibility in its operation. You will note in the material blocked out in red and marked with a star that my membership on the city council was mentioned. In neither piece of material was there any campaign language or any request for support or votes. Please advise whether or not all or any portion of the costs of these two pieces of material should be declared as campaign contributions in the forthcoming city council election in the City of Costa Mesa.
sections.conclusion: If the two publications to which you refer were not published for the purpose of influencing the voters on behalf of your candidacy, you need not report the cost associated with either publication. If, however, they were published for a political purpose, you would be required to report the cost of the jewelry store advertisement as a campaign expenditure and the cost of the bank advertisement as an in-kind contribution. Moreover, if the cost of the bank advertisement was more than $500 and this cost was incurred for a political purpose, the bank would be a committee within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013(b) and required to file campaign statements pursuant to Government Code Sections 84200, et seq. Any doubt as to whether the publications were motivated by a political purpose should be resolved in favor of concluding that there was such a purpose since the relevant statutory provision defines an expenditure as any "payment..., unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." Government Code Section 82025.
sections.question_synthetic: Should an incumbent city council candidate report the costs of a bank advertisement and a business brochure mentioning his council position as campaign contributions or expenditures when neither contains explicit campaign language or vote requests?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The publications must be reported if they were published to influence voters on behalf of the candidate's candidacy, with the bank advertisement treated as an in-kind contribution and potentially triggering committee status if costs exceed $500. When in doubt about political purpose, the determination should favor reporting since expenses are presumed made for political purposes unless clearly otherwise motivated.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal advice letter (not a formal opinion per Government Code Section 83114). Internal FPPC memo indicates request No. 75-222 was not to be treated as formal. Document contains three parts: incoming request (Dec 26), internal routing memo (12/31/75), and FPPC response letter (Jan 16, 1976). Extraction focuses on the formal advice letter response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: I am enclosing a copy of an advertisement which was paid for by South Coast National Bank, of which I am a director. The advertisement appeared well before interest was generated in the city council race in our city, however, a reading of this add mentions my position as a member of the city council. In addition, I am enclosing a copy of a brochure recently published as an announcement to our many friends and customers of a business reorganization which we recently undertook to give my son, Robert, a greater responsibility in its operation. You will note in the material blocked out in red and marked with a star that my membership on the city council was mentioned. In neither piece of material was there any campaign language or any request for support or votes. Please advise whether or not all or any portion of the costs of these two pieces of material should be declared as campaign contributions in the forthcoming city council election in the City of Costa Mesa.

CONCLUSION: If the two publications to which you refer were not published for the purpose of influencing the voters on behalf of your candidacy, you need not report the cost associated with either publication. If, however, they were published for a political purpose, you would be required to report the cost of the jewelry store advertisement as a campaign expenditure and the cost of the bank advertisement as an in-kind contribution. Moreover, if the cost of the bank advertisement was more than $500 and this cost was incurred for a political purpose, the bank would be a committee within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013(b) and required to file campaign statements pursuant to Government Code Sections 84200, et seq. Any doubt as to whether the publications were motivated by a political purpose should be resolved in favor of concluding that there was such a purpose since the relevant statutory provision defines an expenditure as any "payment..., unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." Government Code Section 82025.

embedding.summary: FPPC provided informal staff advice to an incumbent Costa Mesa city council candidate regarding whether advertising costs from a bank and a business brochure should be reported as campaign contributions or expenditures, concluding that reporting is required if the materials were published with political purpose.
